audit_status.source_base: Greek Gospel text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for persistent widow, Pharisee/tax collector, children, rich ruler, third passion prediction, and blind man near Jericho.

verses.restored: He spoke a parable to them about the necessity always to pray and not lose heart.
He said, In a certain city there was a judge who did not fear the Divine and did not respect humans.
There was a widow in that city, and she kept coming to him, saying, Give me justice against my opponent.
For a time he was not willing; but afterward he said within himself, Even though I do not fear the Divine or respect humans,
yet because this widow causes me trouble, I will give her justice, so that she may not finally come and wear me out.
The Lord said, Hear what the judge of injustice says.
Will not the Divine make justice for his chosen ones who cry to him day and night, and will he delay long over them?
I say to you, he will make justice for them quickly. Yet when the Son of Humanity comes, will he find trust on the earth?
He also spoke this parable to some who trusted in themselves that they were righteous and treated the rest as nothing.
Two people went up into the temple to pray, one a Pharisee and the other a tax collector.
The Pharisee, standing by himself, prayed these things: Divine, I thank you that I am not like the rest of people: extortioners, unjust, adulterers, or even like this tax collector.
I fast twice a week; I tithe all that I acquire.
But the tax collector, standing far off, was not even willing to lift his eyes to heaven, but struck his chest, saying, Divine, be merciful to me, the sinner/misaligned one.
I say to you, this one went down to his house justified rather than the other; because everyone exalting oneself will be humbled, but the one humbling oneself will be exalted.
They were bringing even infants to him so that he might touch them. Seeing this, the disciples rebuked them.
But Jesus called them to him, saying, Let the children come to me and do not hinder them, for of such is the reign of the Divine.
Amen, I say to you: whoever does not receive the reign of the Divine like a child will certainly not enter it.
A certain ruler questioned him, saying, Good Teacher, what shall I do to inherit age-long life?
Jesus said to him, Why do you call me good? No one is good except the Divine alone.
You know the commands: Do not commit adultery, do not murder, do not steal, do not bear false witness, honor your father and mother.
He said, All these I have guarded from youth.
Hearing this, Jesus said to him, One thing you still lack: sell all that you have and distribute to poor people, and you will have treasure in the heavens; then come, follow me.
But hearing these things, he became deeply grieved, for he was exceedingly rich.
Seeing him, Jesus said, How difficult it is for those having wealth to enter the reign of the Divine.
For it is easier for a camel to enter through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the reign of the Divine.
Those hearing said, Then who is able to be saved/healed?
He said, The things impossible with humans are possible with the Divine.
Peter said, Look, we left our own things and followed you.
He said to them, Amen, I say to you: there is no one who has left house, wife, brothers, parents, or children because of the reign of the Divine,
who will not receive many times more in this time, and in the coming age, age-long life.
Taking the twelve, he said to them, Look, we are going up to Jerusalem, and all the things written through the prophets about the Son of Humanity will be completed.
For he will be handed over to the nations, mocked, insulted, and spit upon.
After flogging him, they will kill him, and on the third day he will rise.
They understood none of these things; this word was hidden from them, and they did not know the things being said.
As he drew near to Jericho, a certain blind person was sitting beside the way, begging.
Hearing a crowd passing through, he inquired what this might be.
They reported to him, Jesus the Nazarene is passing by.
He cried out, saying, Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me.
Those going before rebuked him so he would be silent, but he cried out much more, Son of David, have mercy on me.
Jesus stood still and commanded him to be brought to him. When he came near, he questioned him,
What do you want me to do for you? He said, Lord, that I may see again.
Jesus said to him, See again; your trust has saved/healed you.
Immediately he saw again and followed him, glorifying the Divine. All the people, seeing, gave praise to the Divine.
